title: Archived Results at a Glance -  Evaluation of the Supporting the Health of Survivors of Family Violence Program 2015-16 to 2018-19 - Canada.ca
content:
## Archived Results at a Glance -  Evaluation of the Supporting the Health of Survivors of Family Violence Program 2015-16 to 2018-19
In 2015,  following a ministerial announcement, the program was launched to improve  health outcomes for survivors of family violence. Funding for this program  supports community-based health promotion interventions for survivors of family  violence, and public health capacity-building initiatives to improve the public  health response for victims of family violence. Projects must incorporate  research in order to add to the evidence available on effective approaches for  improving health outcomes and inform policies and programs. The program uses  multi-sectoral partnerships to help integrate efforts across  organizations.
$21.6 M - 2015-16  to 2018-19
This evaluation  assessed the activities of the Supporting the Health of Survivors of Family  Violence Program for the period of April 2015 to March 2019. This is the first  evaluation of the program. OAE applied a SGBA+ lens, where appropriate.

## What the evaluation found
- Family violence is an important public  health issue affecting Canadians, with some populations more at risk than  others. It can have long-term effects on physical and mental health.
- Although it is early in the program’s  lifecycle, the 21 funded projects are having an impact: Projects are reaching survivors of  family violence through funded interventions. They are also providing  professionals with training sessions, webinars, and workshops. There is anecdotal evidence that  participants have increased their knowledge and skills, or have changed their  behaviours and practices.
- Projects are reaching survivors of  family violence through funded interventions. They are also providing  professionals with training sessions, webinars, and workshops.
- There is anecdotal evidence that  participants have increased their knowledge and skills, or have changed their  behaviours and practices.
- Partnerships have helped generate and  share knowledge widely. In particular, the Knowledge Hub project at Western  University has created a community of practice involving researchers, funded  projects, and PHAC staff. It is providing a mechanism for information sharing,  network building, and problem solving on common issues. This model has been  adapted by other PHAC programs, such as the Mental Health Promotion Innovation  Fund.
- Intervention research being undertaken  as part of the funded projects provides evidence on effective approaches to  inform decision making about scale-up and sustainability.
- Early successes in helping survivors and  health professionals gain knowledge and skills have led to some projects being  scaled up and supported by other levels of government. Sustainability is also  being achieved through new resources to inform the practices of health  professionals.
- The Knowledge Hub and associated       community of practice have been essential to the program’s success. By       creating a mechanism to bring project representatives together to learn       from each other, the Knowledge Hub has really engaged funding recipients       and enabled them to generate and share knowledge to a greater extent than       has typically been possible for similar programs. As a result, this       approach is now being applied in other grants and contributions programs       in PHAC’s Health Promotion and Chronic Disease Prevention Branch.
- Partnerships have been an essential       factor in the early success of the program, notably: multi-sectoral partnerships have        given projects more resources to achieve short-term objectives, as well        as making the possibility of scaling up more realistic; and research and academic partners have        enabled community organizations to better understand project outcomes and        determine whether scale-up and sustainability are worth pursuing.
- multi-sectoral partnerships have        given projects more resources to achieve short-term objectives, as well        as making the possibility of scaling up more realistic; and
- research and academic partners have        enabled community organizations to better understand project outcomes and        determine whether scale-up and sustainability are worth pursuing.
